content.text: Release Train Cadence and Planning Rituals

A release train is a fixed-cadence delivery mechanism: changes that are ready by the departure time get on the train; changes that miss it wait for the next one. Unlike on-demand releases where each feature ships as soon as it is ready, a train batches changes and ships them together on a predictable schedule.

For GenAI applications, trains are particularly valuable because non-deterministic behavior takes time to surface. A prompt change that looks correct in isolation may only show edge-case failures after extended soak in staging. The two-week cadence gives that time, and the freeze gate ensures the window is used for stabilization rather than last-minute additions.

attrs.alt: Release train 2-week cadence with development window, freeze, stabilize, deploy, and retro phases showing planning rituals and GenAI adaptations

content.text: Why Trains Work Well for GenAI
Continuous deployment — shipping every merged PR immediately — is the default for conventional services. For GenAI applications, it creates problems:
Eval results lag behind deployments.
A full eval suite takes 20–60 minutes. With continuous deployment, the next change is already in production before the previous one has a confirmed eval score.
Non-determinism requires soak time.

A prompt that passes all deterministic test cases may still show rare failure modes in staging after hundreds of real-world queries. A 48-hour staging soak catches these; same-day deployment does not.
Safety review needs a stable target.
The safety team reviews the diff between the current production version and the proposed release. If the diff keeps growing because new commits land every hour, the review window never closes.
Rollback attribution is clearer.
When a train introduces a regression, you roll back the entire train — a single known artifact — rather than bisecting which of ten independent deployments caused the issue.
The Four Phases of a Two-Week Train
Phase 1: Development Window (Mon W1 to Wed W2)

The first nine days are the development window. All feature work — code changes, prompt updates, model version pins, retrieval improvements — must be merged before Wednesday of week two. The sprint planning ritual at the start of the window sets the scope, assigns eval targets, and updates the risk register:

## Sprint Planning Checklist (Mon W1)

- [ ] Scope: list PRs targeting this train
- [ ] Eval targets: for each prompt/model change, declare expected accuracy delta
  - e.g., "PR #142 (new system prompt): accuracy +2%, safety neutral"
- [ ] Risk register: any known regressions from last train?
- [ ] Capacity: confirm safety reviewer available for Fri W2 eval review
- [ ] Dependencies: any model provider changes expected this sprint?
- [ ] Freeze reminder: Slack message goes out Wed W2 at 9am UTC
Phase 2: Feature Freeze (Wed W2)

At freeze time, the release branch is cut from main. No new feature PRs can merge to the release branch — only bug fixes and hotfixes for issues found during stabilization. The freeze meeting reviews outstanding PRs:

# scripts/cut_release_branch.py
"""
Cut the release branch at freeze time.
Run by the release manager at the freeze meeting.
"""
import subprocess, datetime, os

def cut_release_branch() -> str:
    today = datetime.date.today()
    # Release name uses ISO week: release/2025-W28
    iso_year, iso_week, _ = today.isocalendar()
    branch = f"release/{iso_year}-W{iso_week:02d}"

    subprocess.run(["git", "checkout", "main"], check=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "pull", "--ff-only", "origin", "main"], check=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "checkout", "-b", branch], check=True)
    subprocess.run(["git", "push", "-u", "origin", branch], check=True)

    print(f"Release branch cut: {branch}")
    print(f"SHA: {subprocess.check_output(['git', 'rev-parse', 'HEAD']).decode().strip()}")
    return branch

if __name__ == "__main__":
    branch = cut_release_branch()
    # Trigger the stabilization CI pipeline on the new branch
    subprocess.run([
        "gh", "workflow", "run", "stabilize.yml",
        "--ref", branch,
    ], check=True)
Phase 3: Stabilization and Eval Review (Thu–Fri W2)

The stabilization window runs the full eval suite — accuracy, safety, mutation — and gives the staging environment 48 hours of soak. The eval review meeting on Friday is the go/no-go decision point. The release manager reads the eval report, the safety team confirms the safety eval passed, and the team votes to approve or defer:
Phase 4: Deploy and Retro (Mon–Tue W3)

Once the eval review approves the train, the two-person approval gate in CI opens. The deploy job runs on Monday morning: blue/green to staging with a 30-minute observation window, then blue/green to production. Tuesday's retrospective closes the loop:
What shipped (list PRs with their eval deltas)
What was deferred (list with reason: missed freeze, eval regression, safety veto)
What new failure modes were found during stabilization — these become new eval cases
MTTR (mean time to resolve) for any incidents since last train
Handling Deferred Items

A PR that misses the freeze does not get cherry-picked into the current train — it waits for the next one. The train departs on schedule regardless of deferred items. This is a hard cultural rule: exceptions erode the entire system.

# .github/branch-protection for release/* branches
# Enforces the no-new-features-after-freeze policy

branch_protection:
  pattern: "release/*"
  required_status_checks:
    - "eval-gate"
    - "safety-gate"
  enforce_admins: true          # even admins cannot bypass
  restrictions:
    users: []
    teams: ["release-managers"]  # only release-managers can push to release/*
  allow_force_pushes: false
  allow_deletions: false
The branch protection rule allows only members of the
release-managers
team to push to a
release/*
branch. A developer who wants to add a hotfix must open a PR against the release branch and get release manager approval — not merge directly.
Out-of-Band Trains for Model Provider Updates
Model providers deprecate API versions on their own schedule, not yours. When a provider announces a deprecation, you need a fast-track train that still runs the full eval suite:
Train Type
Trigger
Development window
Eval required
Approval
Standard
Scheduled (every 2 weeks)
9 days
Full suite
2-person
Hotfix (SEV-1)
Production incident
Hours
Fast subset only
VP + on-call
Model update
Provider deprecation notice
3–5 days
Full suite
2-person + safety
Security patch
CVE in dependency
1 day
Safety + fast subset
2-person
Tracking Train Metrics
Three metrics signal whether the train cadence is healthy:

# scripts/train_metrics.py
import json, boto3
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def train_health_report(train_id: str) -> dict:
    """Compute health metrics for a completed train."""
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")

    def load(key: str) -> dict:
        return json.loads(s3.get_object(
            Bucket="myco-release-metadata", Key=key)["Body"].read())

    train = load(f"trains/{train_id}/metadata.json")
    eval_results = load(f"trains/{train_id}/eval_summary.json")

    planned_prs = set(train["planned_prs"])
    shipped_prs = set(train["shipped_prs"])
    deferred_prs = planned_prs - shipped_prs

    freeze_ts = datetime.fromisoformat(train["freeze_time"])
    deploy_ts = datetime.fromisoformat(train["production_deploy_time"])
    cycle_time_days = (deploy_ts - freeze_ts).days

    return {
        "train_id": train_id,
        # 1. Throughput: % of planned items that shipped
        "throughput": len(shipped_prs) / max(len(planned_prs), 1),
        "deferred_count": len(deferred_prs),
        "deferred_reasons": train.get("deferral_reasons", {}),

        # 2. Eval stability: accuracy delta vs previous train
        "accuracy_delta": eval_results["accuracy_delta"],
        "safety_delta": eval_results["safety_delta"],

        # 3. Cycle time: freeze to production (target: 5 business days)
        "freeze_to_prod_days": cycle_time_days,
    }
Throughput < 70%
consistently means the development window is too short or the freeze gate too strict — revisit the cadence.
Accuracy delta negative for 3+ trains
signals a structural degradation — check if prompt drift or retrieval quality decline is occurring between trains.
Freeze-to-prod > 7 days
indicates the stabilization phase is catching too many late defects — move the freeze gate earlier or increase pre-freeze eval coverage.
Prompt-Specific Freeze Gate

Prompt changes are frozen earlier than code changes because they need more soak time. A prompt that works correctly in 99% of cases may still have rare failure modes that only appear after hundreds of queries. The prompt freeze gate on Wednesday of week one gives those failure modes nine days to surface in the feature sandbox before the main freeze:

# .github/workflows/prompt-freeze-check.yml
# Runs on every push to release/* branches after Wed W1
name: Prompt Freeze Enforcement

on:
  push:
    branches: ["release/*"]
    paths: ["prompts/**"]

jobs:
  check-prompt-freeze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Check if prompt freeze is in effect
        run: |
          FREEZE_FILE=".github/release-state.json"
          PROMPT_FROZEN=$(jq -r '.prompt_frozen' $FREEZE_FILE)

          if [ "$PROMPT_FROZEN" = "true" ]; then
            echo "ERROR: Prompt freeze is in effect for this train."
            echo "Prompt changes are not permitted after Wed W1."
            echo "Defer this change to the next train."
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Prompt freeze not yet in effect. Change allowed."

      - name: Post freeze warning on PR
        if: failure()
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: "Blocked by prompt freeze. This PR modifies prompt files after the prompt freeze date. It will be included in the next train.",
            });

{
  "train_id": "2025-W28",
  "development_window_start": "2025-07-07T09:00:00Z",
  "prompt_freeze_time": "2025-07-09T17:00:00Z",
  "code_freeze_time": "2025-07-16T17:00:00Z",
  "eval_review_time": "2025-07-18T14:00:00Z",
  "deploy_time": "2025-07-21T09:00:00Z",
  "prompt_frozen": true,
  "code_frozen": false,
  "approved": false,
  "deployed": false
}
The
release-state.json
file in
.github/
is updated by the release manager at each phase transition. The freeze enforcement workflow reads it to decide whether to block or allow prompt and code changes.
Communicating Train Status to Stakeholders

Stakeholders outside the engineering team — product, leadership, support — need to know what is in the current train and when it will ship. A generated train summary posted to Slack at each phase transition keeps everyone aligned without manual status updates:

Call this script from each phase-transition CI job so the Slack channel always reflects the current state of the release train. Product managers, support leads, and on-call engineers subscribe to the release channel and know the exact status without having to ask the engineering team.
Eval Suite Growth Between Trains

The retro's most durable output is new eval cases. Every failure mode caught during stabilization — a prompt edge case, a retrieval gap, an unexpected model response — should become a new test case before the next train begins:

# scripts/add_regression_case.py
"""
Add a new eval case from a failure observed during this train's stabilization.
Run during or after the retro meeting.
"""
import json, uuid, pathlib, subprocess

FIXTURE_DIR = pathlib.Path("tests/eval_fixtures")

def add_case(
    query: str,
    expected_behavior: str,
    actual_behavior: str,
    train_id: str,
    category: str = "regression",
) -> dict:
    case = {
        "id": str(uuid.uuid4()),
        "query": query,
        "expected_behavior": expected_behavior,
        "actual_behavior_at_discovery": actual_behavior,
        "category": category,
        "discovered_in_train": train_id,
        "added_by": "retro",
    }
    path = FIXTURE_DIR / f"{case['id']}.json"
    path.write_text(json.dumps(case, indent=2))

    # Open a PR so the case is reviewed before being merged
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", str(path)], check=True)
    subprocess.run([
        "git", "commit", "-m",
        f"eval: add regression case from train {train_id} ({category})"
    ], check=True)

    print(f"Case added: {path}")
    return case

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    add_case(
        query=sys.argv[1],
        expected_behavior=sys.argv[2],
        actual_behavior=sys.argv[3],
        train_id=sys.argv[4],
    )

Over time, the eval suite grows train-by-train, with each cycle's discovered failures preventing the same failure from reaching production in a future train. Track the suite size as a metric: a suite that isn't growing means the retro is not producing new cases, which usually means stabilization is not surfacing new failure modes — or the retro is being skipped.
Summary
A two-week release train provides the soak time and stability window that GenAI applications need — non-deterministic behavior surfaces during the 48-hour staging soak before production.
Four phases structure the train: development window, feature freeze, stabilization with eval review, and deploy with retro.
Planning rituals at each phase transition (sprint planning, freeze meeting, eval review, retro) keep the team aligned and the safety team in the loop.
Items that miss the freeze wait for the next train — no exceptions. The train departs on schedule.
Out-of-band train types (hotfix, model update, security patch) each have their own abbreviated cadence but never skip the eval gate.
